title: Cat or Kitten Vomiting White Worms: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your kitten is vomiting white worms, it is likely roundworms or tapeworms. Learn why this happens, the urgency level, and how your vet can help.
content_html:
What is Happening to My Kitten?

Finding white worms in your kitten's vomit is a common but understandably distressing experience for any pet owner. This typically indicates a parasitic infestation, most likely roundworms or tapeworms. Roundworms often look like strands of spaghetti, while tapeworms may appear as small, flat, rice-like segments. These parasites live in the intestinal tract and can be expelled if the population becomes too large.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a kitten vomiting worms is Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it does require prompt veterinary attention within the next 24 to 48 hours. Parasites can deprive your kitten of essential nutrients, lead to anemia, or even cause intestinal blockages if left untreated. However, if your kitten is also extremely lethargic, has a severely distended abdomen, or is unable to keep any liquids down, you should contact an emergency clinic immediately.

Why Taking a Photo Helps

When you call or visit your veterinarian, having a clear photo of the worms (or the vomit itself) is incredibly helpful for triage. Different types of parasites require specific medications; a dewormer for roundworms will not work for tapeworms. A visual reference allows your vet to identify the species quickly, ensuring your kitten receives the correct treatment without delay.

faqs.question: Can humans catch worms from their cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, some feline parasites like roundworms are zoonotic, meaning they can be transmitted to humans. It is important to wash your hands thoroughly after handling your kitten or cleaning the litter box.

faqs.question: Should I use an over-the-counter dewormer?
faqs.answer: It is highly recommended to wait for a veterinary diagnosis. Many over-the-counter products are ineffective against certain species or may be unsafe for very young kittens. Your vet will provide a prescription-strength treatment tailored to your pet's weight and needs.

faqs.question: How did my indoor kitten get worms?
faqs.answer: Kittens often contract worms from their mother’s milk or across the placenta before birth. They can also get them from ingesting infected fleas or coming into contact with contaminated soil brought in on shoes.
